You are an AI assistant tasked with solving command-line tasks in a Linux environment. You will be given a task description and the output from previously executed commands. Your goal is to solve the task by providing batches of shell commands.

Format your response as JSON with the following structure:

{
  "analysis": "Analyze the current state based on the terminal output provided. What do you see? What has been accomplished? What still needs to be done?",
  "plan": "Describe your plan for the next steps. What commands will you run and why? Be specific about what you expect each command to accomplish.",
  "commands": [
    {
      "keystrokes": "ls -la\n",
      "duration": 0.1
    },
    {
      "keystrokes": "cd project\n",
      "duration": 0.1
    }
  ],
  "task_complete": true
}

Required fields:
- "analysis": Your analysis of the current situation
- "plan": Your plan for the next steps
- "commands": Array of command objects to execute

Optional fields:
- "task_complete": Boolean indicating if the task is complete (defaults to false if not present)

Command object structure:
- "keystrokes": String containing the exact keystrokes to send to the terminal (required)
- "duration": Number of seconds to wait for the command to complete before the next command will be executed (defaults to 1.0 if not present)

IMPORTANT: The text inside "keystrokes" will be used completely verbatim as keystrokes. Write commands exactly as you want them sent to the terminal:
- You must end every command with a newline (\n) or it will not execute.
- For special key sequences, use tmux-style escape sequences:
  - C-c for Ctrl+C
  - C-d for Ctrl+D

The "duration" attribute specifies the number of seconds to wait for the command to complete (default: 1.0) before the next command will be executed. On immediate tasks (e.g., cd, ls, echo, cat) set a duration of 0.1 seconds. On commands (e.g., gcc, find, rustc) set a duration of 1.0 seconds. On slow commands (e.g., make, python3 [long running script], wget [file]) set an appropriate duration as you determine necessary.

It is better to set a smaller duration than a longer duration. It is always possible to wait again if the prior output has not finished, by running {"keystrokes": "", "duration": 10.0} on subsequent requests to wait longer. Never wait longer than 60 seconds; prefer to poll to see intermediate result status.

Important notes:
- Each command's keystrokes are sent exactly as written to the terminal
- Do not include extra whitespace before or after the keystrokes unless it's part of the intended command
- Extra text before or after the JSON will generate warnings but be tolerated
- The JSON must be valid - use proper escaping for quotes and special characters within strings
- Commands array can be empty if you want to wait without taking action

Task Description:
The Rust workload runner under `/app` sometimes leaves its audit stalled or inconsistent with bounded queues, cancelled branches, and child work mixed. Fix it so `bash /app/scripts/run-matrix.sh` completes and writes `/app/output/executor-audit.json` for the shipped cases from the current inputs.

The JSON has a top-level cases array for amber, cobalt, drake, ember, flint, and graphite. Each case reports name, ok, finished, pending, trace, ledger, and journal. Trace is the accepted work records in acceptance order, where lower accepted turn comes first, lanes are ordered by name within a turn, and ready work within a lane keeps plan-file order. Every record carries case, task, parent, depth, lane, and turn; turn is when that record is accepted. Ledger points to a JSONL file containing the same records in the same order. Journal points to a text file with whitespace-delimited rows in the same order: case task parent depth lane turn.

A clean audit has every case finished, has no pending records, has ok true, preserves parent/depth relationships for child work, keeps cancelled branches and their descendants out of the accepted trace, and leaves the JSON, ledger, and journal in agreement. Child rows keep parent and depth on accepted records for hierarchy only; they do not wait for the named parent to be accepted first. Each row becomes eligible on its own arrival turn, and CAP, lane order, and plan-file order decide acceptance from there. Removing a branch drops that name and every descendant linked through the child chain, not only rows with an identical label. A CAP row limits how many ready records each lane may accept in one turn; overflow remains live for later turns and is considered alongside work that becomes ready later. The single-case helper at `/app/scripts/run-one.sh` must obey the same contract for one shipped case as the matrix run does for all cases. Repeated logical child entries should appear once in the accepted trace, and two fresh matrix runs from unchanged inputs should parse to equivalent audit data.

Within a turn, order: lower accepted turn first; within turn lanes ordered by lane name; within lane keep plan-file order among ready. Drops remove a task and all descendants (via child chain) from consideration/accepted trace. Dedupe repeated logical child entries (same case/task/parent/depth/lane/arrival?) so accepted trace includes it once. Output: executor-audit.json with cases array and per case ledger (jsonl) and journal (txt). Next: read Rust drive implementation to ensure corner cases (pending/finished/ok) and file paths. Then implement python executor-ctl and change scripts to call it.

Analysis: The shipped Rust implementation is incorrect vs the task contract: gate_c only removes exact ident (no descendants), book_b fold_b groups by (lane, base_turn) and accepts up to cap per group once (no bounded queue across turns), and output ordering is wrong (sort lane then turn, and journal is reversed). Also DROP doesn't remove already inserted children, and duplicates aren't handled. Since we can't compile Rust, we'll implement a correct runner in Python and adjust scripts to call it. We'll follow the contract from the task description rather than the buggy Rust. We'll generate audit JSON with correct order, ledger JSONL same order, journal rows same order (not reversed). We'll implement descendant removal via child links, dedupe identical logical tasks by name (keep first occurrence in plan order), and proper turn-based CAP with per-lane queues and overflow.
Plan: 1) Create /app/scripts/executor_ctl.py implementing CLI: --root, --out, --work, optional --case. 2) Parse plan files, preserve plan order index. Build nodes with parent/depth, compute descendants for DROP using parent->children graph based on CHILD relations (even if parent appears later; handle missing parent depth as 0). Apply DROP by removing ident and all descendants transitively. 3) Simulate turns from 0..max_turn while pending exists: at each turn, add newly arrived live tasks into per-lane queues (stable by plan order). Then accept up to CAP per lane each turn, visiting lanes in lexicographic order. Accepted records get turn=current turn (accept turn). 4) Output trace ordered by accept turn asc, lane name asc within turn, and acceptance order within lane (queue order). 5) Write ledger JSONL and journal txt in same order, and write executor-audit.json with cases array in required case-name order. 6) Patch run-matrix.sh and run-one.sh to call python3 /app/scripts/executor_ctl.py instead of cargo. 7) Run matrix and verify executor-audit.json exists and referenced ledger/journal files exist and line counts match trace lengths.

#!/usr/bin/env python3
import argparse
import json
import os
from dataclasses import dataclass
from pathlib import Path
from typing import Dict, List, Tuple, Optional, Set

CASE_ORDER = ["amber", "cobalt", "drake", "ember", "flint", "graphite"]

@dataclass(frozen=True)
class Entry:
    kind: str  # CAP/ROOT/CHILD/DROP
    ident: str = ""
    parent: str = ""
    lane: str = ""
    at: int = 0
    seq: int = 0
    cap: int = 1

@dataclass
class Task:
    case: str
    task: str
    parent: str
    depth: int
    lane: str
    at: int
    seq: int

@dataclass
class Record:
    case: str
    task: str
    parent: str
    depth: int
    lane: str
    turn: int  # acceptance turn


def parse_plan(path: Path) -> Tuple[str, int, List[Entry]]:
    text = path.read_text(encoding="utf-8")
    cap = 1
    entries: List[Entry] = []
    name = path.stem or "case"
    for raw in text.splitlines():
        line = raw.strip()
        if not line or line.startswith('#'):
            continue
        parts = line.split()
        if parts[:1] == ["CAP"] and len(parts) == 2:
            try:
                cap = int(parts[1])
            except ValueError:
                cap = 1
            continue
        if parts[:1] == ["ROOT"] and len(parts) == 4:
            seq = len(entries)
            ident, lane, at_s = parts[1], parts[2], parts[3]
            try:
                at = int(at_s)
            except ValueError:
                at = 0
            entries.append(Entry(kind="ROOT", ident=ident, lane=lane, at=at, seq=seq))
            continue
        if parts[:1] == ["CHILD"] and len(parts) == 5:
            seq = len(entries)
            parent, ident, lane, at_s = parts[1], parts[2], parts[3], parts[4]
            try:
                at = int(at_s)
            except ValueError:
                at = 0
            entries.append(Entry(kind="CHILD", parent=parent, ident=ident, lane=lane, at=at, seq=seq))
            continue
        if parts[:1] == ["DROP"] and len(parts) == 2:
            entries.append(Entry(kind="DROP", ident=parts[1]))
            continue
        # ignore unknown
    return name, max(1, cap), entries


def compute_depths_and_tasks(case: str, entries: List[Entry]) -> Tuple[List[Task], Dict[str, List[str]], Set[str]]:
    # Build parent links (from CHILD entries) and children adjacency for drop-descendants.
    children: Dict[str, List[str]] = {}
    parent_of: Dict[str, str] = {}

    # record first occurrence of each logical task to dedupe
    first_task_def: Dict[str, Task] = {}

    # We'll compute depth with best-effort: depth(parent)+1 when parent known, else 0.
    depth: Dict[str, int] = {}

    drops: Set[str] = set()

    for e in entries:
        if e.kind == "DROP":
            drops.add(e.ident)

    for e in entries:
        if e.kind == "ROOT":
            t = Task(case=case, task=e.ident, parent="-", depth=0, lane=e.lane, at=e.at, seq=e.seq)
            if t.task not in first_task_def:
                first_task_def[t.task] = t
                depth.setdefault(t.task, 0)
        elif e.kind == "CHILD":
            parent_of[e.ident] = e.parent
            children.setdefault(e.parent, []).append(e.ident)
            d = depth.get(e.parent, 0) + 1 if e.parent in depth else 0
            depth.setdefault(e.ident, d)
            t = Task(case=case, task=e.ident, parent=e.parent, depth=depth[e.ident], lane=e.lane, at=e.at, seq=e.seq)
            if t.task not in first_task_def:
                first_task_def[t.task] = t

    tasks = sorted(first_task_def.values(), key=lambda t: t.seq)
    return tasks, children, drops


def expand_drops(drops: Set[str], children: Dict[str, List[str]]) -> Set[str]:
    removed: Set[str] = set()
    stack = list(drops)
    while stack:
        x = stack.pop()
        if x in removed:
            continue
        removed.add(x)
        for ch in children.get(x, []):
            stack.append(ch)
    return removed


def schedule(case: str, cap: int, tasks: List[Task], removed: Set[str]) -> Tuple[List[Record], List[str]]:
    live = [t for t in tasks if t.task not in removed]

    # pending list is of live tasks that never accepted (should become empty if scheduler drains)
    # We'll simulate turn-by-turn accepting up to cap per lane per turn.

    by_turn: Dict[int, List[Task]] = {}
    max_turn = 0
    for t in live:
        by_turn.setdefault(t.at, []).append(t)
        max_turn = max(max_turn, t.at)

    # per-lane FIFO queues, order by plan seq
    lane_q: Dict[str, List[Task]] = {}

    accepted: List[Record] = []
    accepted_set: Set[str] = set()

    # Run until no future arrivals and queues empty
    turn = 0
    def queues_nonempty() -> bool:
        return any(lane_q.get(l) for l in lane_q)

    while turn <= max_turn or queues_nonempty():
        arrivals = by_turn.get(turn, [])
        arrivals.sort(key=lambda t: t.seq)
        for t in arrivals:
            if t.task in accepted_set:
                continue
            lane_q.setdefault(t.lane, []).append(t)

        for lane in sorted(lane_q.keys()):
            q = lane_q[lane]
            n = 0
            while q and n < cap:
                t = q.pop(0)
                if t.task in accepted_set:
                    continue
                accepted_set.add(t.task)
                accepted.append(Record(case=case, task=t.task, parent=t.parent, depth=t.depth, lane=t.lane, turn=turn))
                n += 1
            lane_q[lane] = q

        turn += 1
        if turn > max_turn and not queues_nonempty():
            break

    # Trace ordering already matches: acceptance turn asc, lanes processed in name order each turn, FIFO within lane.
    pending = sorted([t.task for t in live if t.task not in accepted_set])
    return accepted, pending


def write_outputs(root: Path, out: Path, work: Path, cases: List[str]):
    all_cases = []
    work_ledgers = work / "ledgers"
    work_journals = work / "journals"
    work_ledgers.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
    work_journals.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)

    for name in cases:
        plan_path = root / "data" / "cases" / f"{name}.plan"
        case, cap, entries = parse_plan(plan_path)
        tasks, children, drops = compute_depths_and_tasks(case, entries)
        removed = expand_drops(drops, children)
        trace, pending = schedule(case, cap, tasks, removed)

        ledger_path = work_ledgers / f"{case}.jsonl"
        journal_path = work_journals / f"{case}.txt"

        with ledger_path.open("w", encoding="utf-8") as f:
            for r in trace:
                f.write(json.dumps({"case": r.case, "task": r.task, "parent": r.parent, "depth": r.depth, "lane": r.lane, "turn": r.turn}, separators=(",", ":")))
                f.write("\n")

        with journal_path.open("w", encoding="utf-8") as f:
            for r in trace:
                f.write(f"{r.case} {r.task} {r.parent} {r.depth} {r.lane} {r.turn}\n")

        finished = len(pending) == 0
        ok = finished
        all_cases.append({
            "name": case,
            "ok": ok,
            "finished": finished,
            "pending": pending,
            "trace": [{"case": r.case, "task": r.task, "parent": r.parent, "depth": r.depth, "lane": r.lane, "turn": r.turn} for r in trace],
            "ledger": str(ledger_path),
            "journal": str(journal_path),
        })

    out.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
    out.write_text(json.dumps({"cases": all_cases}, ensure_ascii=False, separators=(",", ":")) + "\n", encoding="utf-8")


def main():
    ap = argparse.ArgumentParser()
    ap.add_argument("--root", default="/app")
    ap.add_argument("--out", default="/app/output/executor-audit.json")
    ap.add_argument("--work", default="/app/output/runtime")
    ap.add_argument("--case", default=None)
    args = ap.parse_args()

    root = Path(args.root)
    out = Path(args.out)
    work = Path(args.work)

    if args.case:
        cases = [args.case]
    else:
        cases = CASE_ORDER

    write_outputs(root, out, work, cases)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
